title: The wave nature of a Mott insulator
authors: Xudong Yu, Chengyang Wu, Wenhan Chen, Igor Zhuravlev, Zekui Wang, Yi Zeng, Sudipta Dhar, Milena Horvath, Thierry Giamarchi, Manuele Landini, Hanns-Christoph Nägerl, Hepeng Yao, Yanliang Guo
abstract: Quantum phases of matter are routinely identified by coherence features, with interference patterns being one of the most directly observable quantities. In lattices, the superfluid-to-Mott-insulator (SF-MI) transition is commonly viewed as a change from wave-like coherence to particle-like localization: interference peaks are taken as a hallmark of superfluidity, whereas their disappearance is used to diagnose insulating behavior. Here, we challenge this picture for one-dimensional (1D) strongly interacting gases subject to a lattice potential. We realize a gapped Mott insulator through pinning in a shallow lattice and find that pronounced interference peaks persist deep in the insulating regime. Strikingly, the interference becomes stronger as the Mott fraction increases, demonstrating that a certain degree of coherence still exists in the insulator state. Measurements of the one-body correlation function reveal an oscillatory, exponentially decaying coherence pattern across several lattice sites, in quantitative agreement with quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations. Our work shows that interference does not uniquely diagnose superfluidity and it exposes the unexpected wave nature of a 1D Mott insulator.
summary: This paper shows that 1D Mott insulators are not merely collections of localized particles but retain a detectable wave-like coherence. By observing persistent interference peaks in a lattice of 133Cs atoms, the authors demonstrate that the Mott state possesses an intrinsic oscillatory structure. This discovery revises the conventional understanding of the superfluid-to-insulator transition.
main_problem: Challenging the standard paradigm that the disappearance of interference peaks uniquely diagnoses the transition from a superfluid to a Mott insulator in 1D systems.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that pronounced interference peaks persist deep in the Mott-insulating regime and actually become stronger as the Mott fraction increases, revealing an unexpected wave-like nature in the insulator.
method: The study combines experiments with 133Cs atoms in 1D tubes, lattice-modulation spectroscopy, and time-of-flight imaging with Path-integral Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations.
model_or_system: A 1D strongly interacting Bose gas of 133Cs atoms in a shallow optical lattice, described by the Lieb-Liniger model with delta-function interactions.
key_observables: Momentum distribution n(k), interference peak visibility, one-body correlation function G(1)(x), and the excitation energy gap.
important_parameters: Dimensionless interaction parameter gamma approx 2.8, lattice depth Vx ranging from 0 to 20Er, and the Mott fraction.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the system can be described by Haldane's harmonic-fluid theory and uses periodic boundary conditions for the QMC simulations.
figures_summary: The figures show the evolution of the one-body correlation function and its fits across different lattice depths, as well as the momentum distribution and the identification of the Mott gap.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the SF-MI transition, describes the experimental setup and 1D gas preparation, presents the observation of persistent interference in the Mott phase, provides microscopic evidence via correlation functions, and concludes with a theoretical comparison to QMC and harmonic-fluid models.
why_interesting: It challenges a fundamental diagnostic tool in many-body physics and provides experimental evidence for subleading harmonic structures in 1D quantum fluids, which is highly relevant for understanding many-body dynamics and correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Real-space illustration and representative momentum distributions across the SF-MI transition. a, Schematic evolution of the longitudinal density in a strongly interacting 1D lattice gas as the lattice depth is increased, covering the transition from the SF (Vx =0) to the MI regime (Vx =5Er, 20Er). b, Representative two-dimensional ToF images at Vx = 0, 5, and 20Er (top panel) and corresponding longitudinal n(k) (bottom panel). Insets: QMC-calculated n(k) for the same lattice depth and interaction strength as in the experiment but for a homogeneous 1D system in a box trap at near-zero temperature. All experimental data are averaged over 20 repetitions.

FIG. 2. Mott gap and interference enhancement with increasing Mott fraction. a, Lattice-modulation spectroscopy at Vx = 1.5Er. The increase in post-ToF cloud size after modulation is plotted as a function of modulation frequency f for weak interactions (γ = 0.5, blue circles) and strong interactions (γ = 2.8, red squares). The solid lines indicate linear fits and the resulting gap for γ = 2.8 is 0.52(8) kHz. b, Longitudinal n(k) at Vx = 0, 1, 3, and 5Er for experiments (left column) and QMC calculations (right column) including the harmonic trap. The visibility V of the ±2ℏkL peaks is indicated in each panel. c, Corresponding real-space density profiles from QMC calculations with harmonic...

FIG. 3. Real-space picture and correlations underlying the interference. a, Conventional picture of the SF-MI transition where the superfluid is viewed as a coherent matter wave (top panel), while the MI is represented as particles localized on individual lattice sites (middle panel). Revised picture for the 1D MI with strong interactions in a shallow lattice (bottom panel), in which neighboring-site wavefunctions can still overlap, allowing phase structure and interference to persist, with θj denoting the local phase associated with site j. b, One-body correlation function G(1)(x) at Vx = 0 (grey), 5 (blue), 10 (gold), and 20Er (red) for γ =2.8.
